title: A unified rotating machinery health management framework leveraging large language models for diverse components, conditions, and tasks

agents.description: RotLLM is a unified multimodal agent that processes vibration signals through a Spectral Folding Network (SFN) encoder and projection layer, then feeds the resulting embeddings into a fine-tuned LLM (Qwen2.5-1.5B with LoRA) to generate natural language health management responses for rotating machinery. It optionally uses RAG to retrieve relevant papers and manuals for enhanced, verifiable responses.

genai_model.finetuning_dataset_description: The LMR (Large-scale Multimodal Rotating Machinery) dataset containing 237,298 vibration signal samples from 568 operating conditions across 12 public datasets, plus 3,000 multi-modal instruction fine-tuning samples. The fine-tuning corpus was generated by multiple LLMs and refined by human domain experts through a voting and optimization process, covering tasks such as anomaly detection, fault diagnosis, maintenance recommendations, and risk analysis.

orchestration.orchestration_pattern_description: RotLLM follows a single-step orchestration pattern. A vibration signal and a textual health management query are provided as input. The SFN encoder processes the signal into a vibration embedding, the projection layer maps it to the LLM's semantic space, and the fine-tuned LLM (optionally augmented with RAG-retrieved context) generates a comprehensive health management response in a single forward pass.

human_integration.human_integration_description: Human experts were involved in the dataset construction phase, reviewing and refining LLM-generated instruction-response pairs through a voting and optimization process. In deployment, human operators submit health management queries and receive natural language diagnostic reports. The system is designed to lower the barrier for non-expert users. A user study with 100 volunteers (experts and non-experts) was conducted to score system outputs via blind testing.

retrieval.application_domain_description: Prognostics and Health Management (PHM) for rotating machinery (bearings and gears), including fault diagnosis, anomaly detection, maintenance recommendations, and risk analysis under diverse operating conditions.
retrieval.problem_description: Existing health management methods for rotating machinery are fragmented: they are tailored to single component types, limited to specific tasks (e.g., only fault diagnosis), and constrained to narrow operating conditions. They lack generalization across diverse conditions and cannot unify multiple PHM tasks (anomaly detection, fault diagnosis, maintenance recommendation) within a single framework. Additionally, large-scale multimodal datasets covering diverse conditions and components are absent.
retrieval.proposed_solution: RotLLM: a unified rotating machinery health management framework that integrates a Spectral Folding Network (SFN) encoder with a fine-tuned large language model (Qwen2.5-1.5B with LoRA). The SFN transforms vibration spectra into physically-informed embeddings by folding the spectrum into multi-channel representations corresponding to low-, mid-, and high-frequency regions. A projection layer maps these embeddings into the LLM's semantic space. A three-stage training strategy (encoder pre-training, projection layer initialization with label embeddings, and LoRA-based instruction fine-tuning) is used. RAG is optionally employed to retrieve relevant domain knowledge. The LMR dataset (237,298 vibration samples, 568 conditions, 12 public datasets, 3,000 multimodal instruction samples) is constructed and open-sourced to support training and evaluation.
retrieval.research_methodology_description: The study conducts controlled experiments comparing RotLLM and its SFN encoder against multiple state-of-the-art baselines on the constructed LMR dataset. Evaluations include fault classification accuracy, F1-score, FPR, FNR, AUC, generalization under unseen conditions (zero-shot on SDUST), robustness under varying SNR levels, ablation studies removing RAG/LoRA/SFN components, hyperparameter sensitivity analyses (number of folds, nf, LoRA rank), and a human user study scoring response quality.

decision_quality_improvement: SFN achieves 0.9913 accuracy and 0.9931 F1-score on the LMR dataset, matching or exceeding state-of-the-art encoders while reducing FLOPs by 15% and parameters by 21% compared to the baseline FCN.
RotLLM (1.55B parameters) achieves a user score of 9.3/10, comparable to or exceeding much larger models like Qwen2.5-32B (8.9/10) and ChatGPT (9.1/10).
Removing RAG reduces user score from 9.3/10 to 8.8/10; removing LoRA reduces it to 8.4/10; using SFN-only label mapping yields only 2.3/10.
SFN reduces FPR by 19% and FNR by 21% compared to the baseline FCN, with both metrics below 1%.

Zero-shot generalization on SDUST dataset demonstrates robust fault classification under unseen operating conditions with spectral folding; removing spectral folding causes significant accuracy decline.
Three-fold SFN configuration reduces FLOPs by ~65% and parameters by ~19% compared to single-channel CNN baseline with only ~0.3% accuracy decrease.
response_time_impact: RotLLM (1.55B) achieves inference in approximately 10 seconds on CPU.
SFN-only label mapping achieves approximately 0.1 seconds inference on CPU.
SFN+ChatGPT requires approximately 20 seconds via Web API.
SFN+Qwen2.5-32B requires approximately 13 seconds on GPU.
reliability_metrics: RotLLM maintains 84% accuracy under strong noise conditions at 0 dB SNR (noise power equals signal power).
RotLLM achieves 66% accuracy under extreme noise at -8 dB SNR (noise power ~6.3x signal power).
RotLLM consistently outperforms the baseline model across all SNR levels.
AUC of 0.9999 achieved by SFN on the LMR test set.
FPR of 0.0055 and FNR of 0.0038 achieved by SFN.

ce_technique_effectiveness.retrieval-augmented-generation: RAG contributes a 0.5-point improvement in user score (from 8.8/10 to 9.3/10), providing specific, verifiable recommendations (e.g., citing specific grease types and maintenance handbooks) that enhance credibility and actionability.
ce_technique_effectiveness.in-context-learning: Instruction fine-tuning via LoRA contributes the largest single improvement of 0.9 points in user score (from 8.4/10 to 9.3/10), enabling the model to form coherent diagnostic narratives and correctly link signal features to health management knowledge.

failure_modes: Without pre-training (Stage 1) or projection layer initialization (Stage 2), training loss does not converge during instruction fine-tuning, resulting in incoherent and factually incorrect PHM responses.

Without LoRA fine-tuning, the model can retrieve relevant facts via RAG but fails to form coherent diagnostic narratives or correctly link signal features to retrieved knowledge, leading to logical errors.
SFN without spectral folding shows increased misjudgments in bearing fault severity levels under unseen operating conditions.
Text-label mapping (SFN Only) discards probabilities of co-existing health states, causing the system to respond only to the dominant fault and miss compound fault conditions.
Higher noise intensity introduces more randomness, widening the discrepancy between training and test data distributions and reducing test accuracy.
limitations: The current projection layer only maps vibration signals to basic health categories in the pre-training stage, potentially missing detailed information such as amplitude and frequency features.

The current method analyzes each vibration segment separately, ignoring temporal connections between segments, limiting performance on time-based tasks like remaining useful life (RUL) prediction or dynamic risk assessment.
The framework has not yet been validated on other industrial domains (e.g., wind turbines, HVAC systems) beyond rotating machinery.
Issues such as sensor aging, data drift, and changing operating conditions are not yet addressed in the current implementation.

baseline_methods: WDCNN (Wide First-layer Deep Convolutional Neural Network)
identified_research_gaps: Current projection layer only maps vibration signals to basic health categories, potentially missing detailed information such as amplitude and frequency features

Current method analyzes each vibration segment separately, ignoring temporal connections between segments, limiting ability to handle time-based tasks like remaining useful life (RUL) prediction or dynamic risk assessment
Need to improve pre-training stage of encoder and projection layers to use richer spectral features from vibration signals
Need to add more input modalities such as infrared images, current signals, and torque data for a more complete view of machine health
Future versions should process sequences of vibration segments to improve performance on time-dependent tasks like RUL prediction
Adaptation to other industries such as wind turbines, HVAC systems by adjusting input data and adding domain-specific knowledge
Need to explore online learning, adaptive normalization, and advanced signal processing to handle sensor aging, data drift, and changing operating conditions
